rulings.Case Title: IN RE: REGINA QUARLES
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Petition To The Court For An Order For Relief From The Provisions Of Government Code 945.4
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, August 13, 2024, Line 2. PETITIONER REGINA QUARLES' Hearing On Petition To The Court For An Order For Relief From The Provisions Of Government Code 945.4. The petition for relief from Government Code 945.4 is ordered off calendar for petitioner's noncompliance with Local Rule 2.7(B) (courtesy copies). The petition may be re-set for a Mon.-Thur. after August 29. Petitioner's papers shall bear the new date. For the 9:30 a.m. Law & Motion calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: RICHARD JAMES MCDOUGALL VS. LYFT, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT SUSAN MARQUEZ'S MOTION TO COMPEL DEPOSITIONS OF GOOGLE'S PMQ OF PROMOTIONS
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, August 13, 2024, Line 1, DEFENDANT SUSAN MARQUEZ's Motion To Compel Depositions Of Google's PMQ Of Promotions. (Part 2 of 2). Moving to the heart of the dispute between Google and Marquez, claims of waiver and lack of specificity (or too much particularity) by Google are not convincing given the discovery principles noted above. This is not a matter even within hailing distance of the facts underlying the decision in Calcor Space Facility, Inc. v. Superior Court, 53 Cal.App.4th 216 (1997). Marquez's subpoena and notice of deposition list both specific and general categories of documents sought and subjects to be covered in the PMQ deposition. If the documents requested have already been produced by Google, it can point that out (which it has not done to date). If not, they should be produced. As for the previous deposition of plaintiff's immediate superior, that does not constitute testimony by Google, which is the whole purpose of a PMQ deposition. The record clearly shows what documents Marquez seeks and the subjects she seeks to explore with a Google corporate representative. For these reasons, and for good cause shown, Marquez's motion for an order compelling non-party Google, LLC to produce documents for examination and a PMQ witness for deposition is GRANTED. Counsel for defendant Marquez shall prepare a proposed order consistent with the foregoing and submit it to psw@hassard.com for review and signature. For the 9:00 a.m. Discovery calendar, all attorneys and parties who stipulate to the Judge Pro Tem and submit to the tentative ruling, hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link (DISCOVERY, DEPARTMENT 525 DAILY AT 9:00 A.M.), or dial the corresponding number and use the meeting ID, and password for Discovery Department 525. **** FOR ALL PARTIES THAT CONTEST THE TENTATIVE RULING OR DO NOT STIPULATE TO THE JUDGE PRO TEM, A PERSONAL APPEARANCE IS REQUIRED. **** Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to psw@hassard.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must e-mail it to the Judge Pro Tem. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. (End of tentative ruling). =(302/RBU).

rulings.Case Title: RICHARD JAMES MCDOUGALL VS. LYFT, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Depositions Of Google'S Pmq Of Promotions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, August 13, 2024, Line 1, DEFENDANT SUSAN MARQUEZ's Motion To Compel Depositions Of Google's PMQ Of Promotions. (Part 1 of 2). Pro Tem Judge Philip Ward, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: It has been well-established in this state since before the enactment of the Civil Discovery Act that the scope and breadth of civil discovery should be read liberally, with any doubts resolved in favor of allowing discovery. As our state Supreme Court observed in its introductory remarks to Williams v. Superior Court, 3 Cal.5th 531, 538 (2017), "In the absence of privilege, the right to discovery in this state is a broad one, to be construed liberally so that parties may ascertain the strength of their case and at trial the truth may be determined." To enable this general principle, trial courts are vested with "wide discretion" to allow (or prohibit) civil discovery. Williams, supra, at 540. When it comes to discovery from non-parties, such as Google, LLC, plaintiff's employer, discovery by way of subpoena may include an oral deposition, the production of records or both. Code of Civil Procedure section 2020.020, et seq. To that end, defendant Marquez served a subpoena and noticed the deposition of Google's PMQ with respect to the company's criteria, standards and procedures governing the promotion of software engineers at and above plaintiff's employment level (L8 to L9). The purpose of this discovery was to test the accuracy of plaintiff's claim that as a result of being rearended by an auto driven by Marquez, he was precluded from or denied promotion at Google, resulting in significant lost future wages. This discovery effort followed Google's production of hundreds of pages of documents requested by Marquez and the deposition of plaintiff's immediate superior who, according to the record, testified about plaintiff, his job performance and, as the superior understood it, the likelihood of plaintiff being promoted from L8 to L9. Against that backdrop, Google takes the position that Marquez has had enough discovery and that any further delving into plaintiff's promotional prospects would be unduly burdensome and an abuse of the deposition and subpoena process. For that reason, it has resisted and vigorously opposes Marquez's motion for an order compelling it to produce a "promotional PMQ" for deposition and produce promotion-related documents listed in both the deposition subpoena and the notice of deposition served by Marquez. It has done so throughout the meet and confer process initiated by Marquez, the sufficiency of which satisfies her obligation to attempt to avoid the need to seek court intervention. (Continued to Part 2). =(302/RBU).

rulings.Case Title: THOMAS LIBBY VS. STATE FARM INSURANCE CO. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO COMPEL RESPONSES TO ADMISSION/INTERROGATORIES
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, August 13, 2024, Line 3, DEFENDANT STATE FARM GENERAL INSURANCE COMPANY's MOTION TO COMPEL RESPONSES TO ADMISSION/INTERROGATORIES. (Part 1 of 2). Pro Tem Judge Philip Ward, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff initiated this action against defendant State Farm Insurance Company arising out of an insurance claim for damaged personal property. The defendant served two sets of discovery on plaintiff: a set of special interrogatories and a Request For Admissions of Genuineness of Documents. Plaintiff did not respond to either discovery effort. Defendant sent one letter to plaintiff seeking responses to which it received no reply. The defendant then filed the two motions now before the Court: one for an order compelling responses to the special interrogatories and the second for an order affirming the genuineness of the documents attached to its Request for Admissions. Plaintiff has not responded to either motion. The defendant seeks sanctions in connection with each motion. Accordingly, for good cause shown, defendant's motion for an order compelling plaintiff to respond, without objections, to the set of special interrogatories served upon him is GRANTED. Similarly, defendant's motion for an order deeming genuine the documents attached to its Request for Admissions is GRANTED. Since defendant's efforts to secure these two orders did not require preparation of reply pleadings, and given their relative ease of preparation, sanctions are GRANTED against plaintiff, appearing in propria persona, in the amount of $800.00, payable within 10 days of the service date of the order referred to herein. Counsel for defendant shall prepare a proposed order consistent with the foregoing decision and email it to psw@hassard.com for review and signature. For the 9:00 a.m. Discovery calendar, all attorneys and parties who stipulate to the Judge Pro Tem and submit to the tentative ruling, hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link (DISCOVERY, DEPARTMENT 525 DAILY AT 9:00 A.M.), or dial the corresponding number and use the meeting ID, and password for Discovery Department 525. **** FOR ALL PARTIES THAT CONTEST THE TENTATIVE RULING OR DO NOT STIPULATE TO THE JUDGE PRO TEM, A PERSONAL APPEARANCE IS REQUIRED. **** (Continued to Part 2). =(302/RBU).

rulings.Case Title: VIA SENTI, A CALIFORNIA 501(C)(3) NONPROFIT VS. CARRIE ALISON MALONEY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses And Production Of Documents To Its First Set Of Requests From Defendant Carrie Alison Maloney And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, August 13, 2024, Line 5, PLAINTIFF VIA SENTI's Motion To Compel Further Responses And Production Of Documents To Its First Set Of Requests From Defendant Carrie Alison Maloney And Request For Sanctions. (Part 1 of 2). Pro Tem Judge Philip Ward, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff has accused defendant Maloney and her business entities of fraudulently creating and/or inflating her experience in charitable fundraising in order to secure a full-time employment contract with plaintiff that paid $150,000 per annum in compensation. Plaintiff alleges Maloney did not have the experience she claimed, did not work full-time on this engagement and failed to deliver any of the promised performances that were the subject of the contract between her and plaintiff. Plaintiff served written discovery on plaintiff that included a Request for Production of Documents. After several rounds of meet and confer activities between counsel, and some limited further document responses by defendant, the parties reached an apparent impasse with respect to documents responsive to Request Nos. 5, 6, 8, 9, 10 and 11 to which defendants had objected on a number of grounds. On July 19, 2024, plaintiff filed a motion for an order compelling defendants to respond to the identified document requests without objections, and requested sanctions in the amount of $7,110. The defendants did not reply to or oppose plaintiff's motion. The Court has one question for plaintiff. The meet and confer email chain (and Mr. Soriano's declaration) reflect agreements among counsel to extend the statutory deadline to file the subject motion to compel to and including July 19, 2024 (a Friday). The motion, however, was not filed until July 22, 2024 (the following Monday). Is there a reason for this delay and/or authority that would allow plaintiff to proceed with the motion even if filed after the agreed-upon deadline of July 19? Is defendants' failure to reply to or oppose the motion to compel material in responding to that question? See, Sexton v. Superior Court, 58 Cal.App.4th 1403, 1410 (1997). For this reason, the Court's tentative decision is "Hearing Required". The motion itself appears well-founded, with plaintiff entitled to the documents sought in the identified requests given their relevance and, if indeed there are genuine confidentiality concerns, defendants' failure to seek a protective order and their failure to respond to the motion to compel. Sanctions in the amount requested would also seem in order, if the Court ends up ruling on the merits of the motion. (Continued to Part 2). =(302/RBU).

rulings.Case Title: JINA PARK VS. JUSTIN P. FONG M.D ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Deeming Admitted Truth Of Facts And Genuineness Of Documents And Imposing Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, August 13, 2024, Line 6, PLAINTIFF JINA PARK's Motion For Order Deeming Admitted Truth Of Facts And Genuineness Of Documents And Imposing Monetary Sanctions. (Part 1 of 2). Pro Tem Judge Philip Ward, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff was employed by the defendant physician as an office worker for a number of months, after having volunteered at the doctor's office previously. Among other causes of action, plaintiff alleges that during the course of her employment, she was treated in a discriminatory manner due to her national origin. The defendant is appearing in propria persona. On March 29, 2024 and then again on April 23, plaintiff served a Request for Admissions seeking admissions of various factual assertions and the genuineness of an attached document, defendant did not provide timely responses. After a letter demanding responses was sent by plaintiff, and the instant motion was filed, on July 30, 2024 defendant submitted responses containing a somewhat defective verification. Although plaintiff had also served other written discovery on defendant to which responses were also late, she has evidently chosen not to address those responses in her motion. As plaintiff notes in her reply memorandum, defendant's defectively verified (and late) responses to the Request for Admissions could be viewed as tantamount to no responses at all. That said, defendant is laboring without an attorney (he certainly could use one) and if plaintiff's motion were granted, the deemed admissions would almost certainly establish liability on defendant's part. Defendant's late responses, however, suggest that his liability is at least questionable at this point, including whether jurisdiction under the Fair Employment and Housing Act, Government Code sections 12940, et seq., is warranted on the facts of this case. Therefore, "in the interests of justice", the Court is disinclined to grant plaintiff's motion at this point in time. Instead, the Court ORDERS defendant to serve upon plaintiff a proper verification of his responses to discovery (see defendant's July 30, 2024 response and exhibits thereto) by August 19, 2024. Plaintiff can continue to prosecute her claims going forward and return to defendant's alleged liability therefor at some later date. For those reasons, plaintiff's motion is DENIED, albeit without prejudice. However, defendant's handling of plaintiff's discovery efforts resulted in plaintiff having to prepare and file the instant motion when all that could have been avoided with timely verified responses. Accordingly, plaintiff's request for sanctions is GRANTED in the amount of $2,685, payable no later than August 30, 2024. (Continued to Part 2). =(302/RBU).

rulings.Case Title: CITIBANK, N.A. VS. JULIO MUNOZ
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Order That Matters In Request For Admission Of Truth Of Facts Bk Deemed Admitted
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, August 13, 2024, Line 7, PLAINTIFF CITIBANK, N.A.'s Motion For Order That Matters In Request For Admission Of Truth Of Facts Bk Deemed Admitted. (Part 1 of 2). Pro Tem Judge Philip Ward, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff alleges that it is the owner of an AMEX credit card account in defendant's name on which an unpaid balance exists in the amount of $7,728.53. Plaintiff has filed a collection action, and defendant has filed an answer. On April 3, 2024 plaintiff served a Request for Admissions on defendant to which defendant has not responded. As a result, plaintiff filed the instant motion for an order deeming the admissions served on defendant admitted. Given defendant's failure to timely respond to the Request for Admissions propounded by plaintiff, not to mention his failure to respond to the instant motion, for good cause shown, plaintiff's motion for an order deeming its Request for Admissions admitted is GRANTED. Plaintiff's counsel is directed to prepare a proposed order consistent with the foregoing and submit it to psw@hassard.com for review and signature. (Continued to Part 2). =(302/RBU).
